name: Zebra Longwing
description: Florida's official state butterfly. Slow, gliding flight with long black wings striped in pale yellow. Roosts communally at night and is unusual in eating pollen, which gives it a long adult lifespan of up to six months.

caterpillar.description: White body with long black spines and black spots; feeds exclusively on passionvine leaves.

notes: Common year-round in Pinellas County. Loves dappled shade and edges of wooded yards.

name: Monarch
description: The iconic orange-and-black butterfly, famous for its multi-generation migration. Florida populations are partially resident thanks to year-round milkweed. Unmistakable, but compare with the similar Queen and Viceroy.

caterpillar.description: Bold yellow, black, and white bands with short black filaments at both ends. Only eats milkweed.

notes: Cut tropical milkweed back to 6 inches in late October to help prevent OE parasite buildup.

name: Queen
description: Milkweed cousin to the Monarch — deeper mahogany-orange with fewer black veins on top. Males have a scent patch on the hindwing. Year-round resident in south Florida.

caterpillar.description: Similar to Monarch but has three pairs of filaments (Monarch only has two) and creamier banding.

notes: Frequently outnumbers Monarchs in suburban Clearwater gardens.

name: Gulf Fritillary
description: Bright orange above with black markings; underside is spectacular, with large silver spots on a brown background. Fast, nervous flyer. A dependable year-round resident.

caterpillar.description: Orange body with rows of long, branched black spines. Harmless despite the spiny look.

notes: Will completely strip passionvines — plant extras just for the caterpillars.

name: Julia Heliconian
description: Long-winged, bright orange butterfly with a black bar on the forewing of males. Floats gracefully through the garden, often with Zebra Longwings and Gulf Fritillaries.

caterpillar.description: Brown and cream with long dark branched spines along the body.

notes: Males are brighter orange; females more muted and washed with brown.

name: Giant Swallowtail
description: North America's largest butterfly — huge black wings with bold yellow bands that cross to form an X on the upper surface. Distinctive sailing flight. A Clearwater backyard regular wherever citrus grows.

caterpillar.description: Famously mimics a bird dropping — brown and white, glossy. When threatened, it extrudes an orange 'osmeterium' that smells strongly of citrus.

notes: Caterpillars (called 'orange dogs') are often found on backyard citrus — leave a few leaves for them.

name: Eastern Tiger Swallowtail
description: Large yellow butterfly with bold black tiger stripes. Females come in a yellow form and a dark black form that mimics the toxic Pipevine Swallowtail.

caterpillar.description: Plump green caterpillar with two large yellow-and-black eyespots behind the head — a classic 'snake mimic' look.

notes: Less common in urban Pinellas than swallowtails like Giant or Black, but drops by to nectar.

name: Black Swallowtail
description: Black with two rows of yellow spots and iridescent blue on the hindwing. Often called the 'parsley worm' butterfly for its kitchen-garden host plants.

caterpillar.description: Green with black bands and yellow spots — unmistakable on herbs. Plant extra parsley, dill, and fennel to share.

notes: Grow a small 'caterpillar patch' of parsley/dill/fennel if you want both herbs and butterflies.

name: Polydamas Swallowtail
description: The 'tailless swallowtail' — velvety black with a yellow band across both wings and no tail. A south Florida specialty; caterpillars are toxic to predators from eating Dutchman's pipe.

caterpillar.description: Dark red-brown to black with rows of orange-red fleshy tubercles. Feeds communally in small groups on Aristolochia.

hostPlants.name: Pipevine (non-toxic native A. serpentaria or A. pentandra preferred)

notes: Avoid giant/exotic Aristolochia gigantea — flowers trap and kill this butterfly's caterpillars.

name: Palamedes Swallowtail
description: Large dark swallowtail with a bright yellow band across each wing and a yellow stripe along the body. Common in wooded and wetland edges of Pinellas County.

caterpillar.description: Green with two large false eyespots; matures into a brown pre-pupal form before chrysalis.

notes: Populations have declined with laurel wilt disease impacting red bays — any healthy bay tree helps.

name: Spicebush Swallowtail
description: Dark swallowtail with striking blue-green iridescent clouds on the hindwings (green in males, blue in females). Mimics the toxic Pipevine Swallowtail.

caterpillar.description: Green (later turning yellow/orange) with two enormous false eyespots — one of the most beloved 'snake caterpillars.'

name: Cloudless Sulphur
description: Large, fast-flying butterfly in pure lemon yellow (males) or pale greenish-yellow with faint markings (females). Very common and often migrates through Florida in large numbers.

caterpillar.description: Either green with a yellow side stripe (on leaves) or yellow with black bands (on flowers) — color depends on which part of the cassia it eats.

notes: Favors deep, red, tubular flowers — a good reason to grow firebush.

name: Orange-barred Sulphur
description: Larger and more golden than the Cloudless Sulphur, with a bright orange bar across the upper forewing and orange along the hindwing margin of males.

caterpillar.description: Green with a yellow lateral stripe, or yellow-orange when feeding on cassia flowers.

name: White Peacock
description: Soft white with faint orange lines and two prominent black eyespots on each wing. Low, fluttering flight close to the ground in open, damp areas and lawn edges.

caterpillar.description: Black with silvery-white spots and branched spines; harmless.

notes: Plant frogfruit as a low-water Florida lawn alternative and you'll get White Peacocks constantly.

name: Atala
description: A small, jewel-like butterfly — velvety black wings dusted with iridescent blue-green, and a bright orange-red abdomen. Once feared extinct in Florida; now recovering thanks to native coontie plantings.

caterpillar.description: Bright red-orange with twin rows of yellow spots — shockingly colorful, warning predators it's toxic.

notes: More common on the east coast of Florida but spreading — plant coontie and they may find you.

name: Painted Lady
description: Orange and black with white spots on the forewing tips and four small eyespots on the hindwing underside. One of the world's most widespread butterflies.

caterpillar.description: Dark with yellow side stripes and short branched spines; builds a silk 'tent' on host leaves.

name: American Lady
description: Very similar to Painted Lady but with two large eyespots on the hindwing underside (Painted Lady has four small ones). Warm orange with rich patterning.

caterpillar.description: Black with thin yellow bands and rows of branched black spines dotted with white.

name: Red Admiral
description: Dark with a bold red-orange band across each wing and white spots on the forewing tips. Fast, territorial flyer often seen perched on fences or tree trunks.

caterpillar.description: Variable — usually black with white dots and yellow sides, spiny. Rolls nettle leaves into a shelter.

name: Viceroy
description: A Monarch mimic — orange with black veins, but with an extra black line arcing across the hindwing and a slightly smaller size. Flap-and-glide flight.

caterpillar.description: Mottled white, brown, and olive — another bird-dropping mimic. Feeds on willow and poplar.

name: Great Southern White
description: Chalky white with a zigzag black border on the forewing. Antennae tips are turquoise-blue — diagnostic. Very common in coastal Pinellas.

caterpillar.description: Yellow-green with four narrow yellow lines down the back and a row of tiny black spots.

notes: Caterpillars can strip backyard brassicas — consider row cover over edible crops if you don't want to share.

name: Cassius Blue
description: Tiny, pale blue butterfly (paler in females). Underside is banded brown and white with two small eyespots. Very fast, often overlooked.

caterpillar.description: Short, flattened, slug-like caterpillar — green with pink or brown tints. Often tended by ants.

notes: If you grow plumbago, look closely — you almost certainly have Cassius Blues.

name: Gray Hairstreak
description: Small gray butterfly with an orange and black eyespot near delicate hair-like tails on each hindwing — a classic 'false head' mimic.

caterpillar.description: Small, slug-like, highly variable in color — green, pink, brown — often matching its host flower.

name: Long-tailed Skipper
description: Brown with iridescent blue-green sheen on the body and wing bases, plus long tail-like extensions on the hindwings. Chunky 'skipper' body and fast, darting flight.

caterpillar.description: Often called the 'bean leafroller' — yellow-green with dark head, folds leaves together as a shelter.

name: Common Buckeye
description: Brown with stunning multicolor eyespots — two on the forewing and two on the hindwing — ringed in black, yellow, and iridescent blue. Often basks on bare ground.

caterpillar.description: Black with orange-and-cream side stripes and rows of short branched spines — tipped with iridescent blue at the base.
